data.title: Air New Zealand Buys Back Over 400,000 Ordinary Shares
data.description:
Air New Zealand bought back 404,923 ordinary shares, according to a Friday filing with the Australian and New Zealand bourses.

The company repurchased 134,248 ordinary shares on the New Zealand stock exchange at an average price of NZ$0.597; 64,133 ordinary shares on the Australian stock exchange at AU$0.55 apiece; and 206,542 ordinary shares acquired off-market from the sovereign through the Minister of Finance at NZ$0.5969 each, the filing stated.

The company's Australian shares were up nearly 1% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Yomiuri: Early Retirement Offers Nearly Double in Japan
data.description:
By Shuntaro Murase

Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

Workforce reductions are being implemented at a growing scale in Japan. The number of offers for early or voluntary retirement so far this year has been nearly double what it was in the same period last year.

Given the pace of the increase, the number throughout the year is anticipated to rival that of 2009, the year following the collapse of Lehman Brothers. Against the backdrop of high U.S. tariffs and concerns over a slowdown in the global economy, even profitable companies, notably, are increasingly opting to reduce their workforces before performance can deteriorate.

According to Tokyo Shoko Research Ltd., the number of early and voluntary retirement offers made by listed companies this year had reached 8,711 as of May 15, nearly double the 4,654 in the same period last year. While the number of companies implementing such measures decreased by eight to 19, there has been an increase in large-scale layoffs, with a high number of retirement offers at each company.

Panasonic Holdings Corp. announced in May that it will cut 5,000 personnel in Japan. It will implement an equal number of layoffs overseas, for a total of about 10,000 eliminated jobs. Japan Display Inc., a maker of small and medium-sized LCDs, will solicit voluntary retirement applications from June to August, aiming to reduce its domestic workforce by about 1,500, or about half of its current employees. Mazda Motor Corp. will seek about 500 of its regular employees aged 50 to 61 to retire.

The scale of personnel reductions is likely to increase significantly in the future. Nissan Motor Co. has announced plans to cut 20,000 jobs worldwide and is considering offering early retirement to employees in its administrative departments in Japan. Renesas Electronics Corp., a major semiconductor company, is reportedly planning to cut hundreds of jobs in Japan and overseas.

60% in the black

In the past, early and voluntary retirement surged during periods when the business environment worsened due to such external factors as the collapse of Lehman Brothers, the Great East Japan Earthquake and the COVID-19 pandemic. However, among the 19 listed companies that have announced or are reportedly planning to implement early and voluntary retirement programs this year, 12, or about 60%, reported unconsolidated net profits in their latest financial results.

Panasonic Holdings reported a consolidated net profit of 366.2 billion yen for the fiscal year that ended March 2025. Regarding the company's job cut plan, President Yuki Kusumi said, "We will not be able to return to growth without making drastic changes to our fixed cost structure."

Many companies now expect performance to deteriorate going forward due to factors such as the high U.S. tariffs and a slowdown in the global economy. According to research by SMBC Nikko Securities Inc., about 40% of companies expect a decline in net profits for the fiscal year ending March 2026.

"To secure medium- to long-term competitiveness, more companies are opting to reduce staff while still profitable," said Kosuke Honma of Tokyo Shoko Research. "There is a possibility of preventive cuts emerging due to the impact of the tariff policies of the administration of U.S. President Donald Trump."

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal were not involved in the creation of this content.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks set for sixth straight weekly gain on listing momentum
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks rose on Friday and were set for a sixth straight weekly gain, underpinned by renewed listing momentum and signs of easing Sino-U.S. trade tensions. China stocks edged up on the day.

** China's blue-chip CSI300 Index inched up 0.3% by the lunch break, while the Shanghai Composite Index gained 0.1%. Hong Kong benchmark Hang Seng (.HIS) was up 0.6%.

** For the week, the Hang Seng Index is up 1.4% so far, while the CSI300 index added 0.9%.

** Hong Kong's listing market showed signs of revival in 2025, buoyed by the strong debut of battery giant CATL , which raised $4.6 billion in the world's largest listing so far this year.

** Shares of pharma giant Jiangsu Hengrui (1276.HK) soared more than 30% in their Hong Kong debut on Friday, lifting sentiment across the healthcare sector. The CSI Healthcare Index (.CSIHC) rose 1.4%.

** BYD shares jumped nearly 4% after reports showed the Chinese automaker sold more electric vehicles in Europe than Tesla . Auto shares tracked gains and were up over 3%. Li Auto's shares rose 3.2%.

** A slew of investment banks including Morgan Stanley and UBS raised their forecasts for the Hang Seng Index this month, citing improved corporate earnings and a thaw in Sino-U.S. trade tensions following a tariff truce.

** Xiaomi's said it will start selling its YU7 electric vehicle in July. Its shares were roughly flat after the news.

data.title: Singapore Court Sanctions PEC Scheme of Arrangement Ahead of Delisting
data.description:
A Singapore court sanctioned the scheme of arrangement for PEC in relation to the acquisition of its entire paid up share capital by Alliance Energy Services, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

Following the decision, trading in shares of the company will be suspended, with effect from May 28, with its delisting to follow on June 19.

data.title: Yomiuri: Japanese Prime Minister Aims to Sell Stockpiled Rice through Contracts
data.description:
By Haruna Fukushima

Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

Japanese Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba on Wednesday revealed that he is looking into changing the method by which stockpiled rice is sold from bidding to negotiated contracts, which would enable the government to choose buyers.

By reviewing the government's method of selling stockpiled rice, the prime minister aims to curb the surge in rice prices.

"The price of rice must be between 3,000 yen and 4,000 yen (per 5 kilograms). We will achieve this price as early as possible," Ishiba said during a debate between party leaders on Wednesday. Ishiba added that he will have to "take responsibility" if the government fails to achieve the goal.

"I agree with the argument that we should shift toward increasing production (of rice)," the prime minister added.

According to the Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries Ministry, the average retail price of rice sold at supermarkets has recently exceeded 4,000 yen per 5 kilograms. Prices remain at a level more than double what they were during the same period last year.

Newly appointed agriculture minister Shinjiro Koizumi revealed during his inaugural press conference on Wednesday that the fourth auction of stockpiled rice, which was scheduled to take place from May 28 to 30, will be cancelled. Koizumi said he has instructed relevant officials to take specific measures, such as drafting terms, to prepare to conclude negotiated contracts.

Currently, the government sells stockpiled rice through open bidding. The competitive process has become one of the reasons auction prices have risen, as the bidders that make the highest offers are awarded contracts in sequence. The details of government's plan are unclear, but in general, the government can select contractors at its discretion under a negotiated contract, taking into account factors such as price. Observers expect if the government succeeds in curbing prices of stockpiled rice, it may lead to a decrease in retail prices.

The government has conducted three auctions of stockpiled rice, leading to the release of 310,000 tons of rice in total. The average auction price was 22,477 yen per 60 kilograms, tax included. Meanwhile, when the government purchased rice harvested in fiscal 2023, the price was 12,829 yen, which is about 10,000 yen less than the auction price. This situation has drawn criticism even from within the Liberal Democratic Party. "It is unfathomable for the government to make a profit (by selling stockpiled rice)," said LDP Research Council Chairperson Itsunori Onodera.

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal were not involved in the creation of this content.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0248 GMT - Palm oil gains in early Asian trade, tracking overnight gains in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade and stronger palm olein prices on the Dalian Commodity Exchange, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Philip Nova expects CPO futures may remain range-bound, with key support at 3,800 ringgit/ton and firm resistance around 3,930 ringgit/ton amid persistent selling. A forming symmetrical triangle signals potential volatility as traders await a clearer catalyst, it adds in a note. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery is up by 37 ringgit to 3,857 ringgit/ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0231 GMT - The market will likely expect the successor for Rio Tinto CEO Jakob Stausholm to be an internal candidate, according to Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. He views iron-ore chief Simon Trott and Chief Commercial Officer Bold Baatar, a former copper boss, as the likely front runners. Like other analysts, McTaggart says the succession plan came as a surprise. "We won't speculate as to why Stausholm is leaving earlier than most would have expected," he says in a note. Citi has a neutral rating and A$130.00 target on Rio Tinto, which is down 1.0% at A$117.64.(rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0229 GMT - Iron ore prices are slightly lower in early Asian trade. While the number of steel mill maintenance rises in China, consumption of iron ore continues to decline, Baocheng Futures says in a research note. Meanwhile, China's iron-ore supply remains abundant, as overseas miners are actively shipping, it says. The fundamentals of iron ore is weakening, weighing on the ferrous metal's prices, it adds. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange edges 0.1% lower at CNY726.0 a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0106 GMT - Copper is steady in early Asian trade. Inventories of key metals, such as copper, have been rising in recent weeks, suggesting that demand has waned, ANZ Research analysts write in a note. However, recent supply side issues could help limit the downside, they say. Energy and commodity trading company Mercuria Energy warned recently that deficits in both the copper concentrate and refined market may develop this year, they add. The three-month LME copper contract is flat at $9,502.00 a ton.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2359 GMT - Gold edges higher in the early Asian session, supported by lingering worries over ballooning U.S. deficits, which typically bolster the safe-haven appeal of the precious metal. President Trump's "big, beautiful bill" passed the House of Representatives on Thursday, paving the way for the bill to go to the Senate. The bill is widely expected to add trillions of dollars to budget deficits in coming years. An absence of new trade 'deals' and continued worries about the U.S.' fiscal trajectory mean that optimism remains hard to find, says Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at IG, in an email. Spot is 0.2% higher at $3,299.98/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2338 GMT - Morgans views recent setbacks at EQ Resources' Mount Carbine tungsten mine in Queensland state as a blip. Production fell by more than 50% in the three months through December due to the availability of process water, according to analyst Chris Brown. Then, seasonal wet weather stymied efforts to bring in consumables, including explosives, from Townsville, and production fell further. "Our expectation is for a return to normal operating conditions for the balance of 2025," says Morgans. "We expect a major turn-around for Mt Carbine, returning to previous mining rates, and benefiting from an increased head grade." Morgans, which rates EQ Resources as a "speculative buy," expects the operation will be cash flow positive. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2323 GMT - The delay in ramping up Fortescue's Iron Bridge project to nameplate capacity disappoints Macquarie analysts, who expect little cost relief from the miner pushing out its production target. "FMG carries a large fixed-cost base at Iron Bridge," the analysts say in a note. "Therefore, we assume the volume cuts flow directly to the bottom line." However, they take a positive view on executive changes, which appear to indicate a refreshed focus on the metals business, they say. "One of our criticisms on FMG's 'FFI' strategy has been it's non-adjacency and limited grounds for genuine competitive advantage," say the analysts. "This move further aligns decarbonization and mining, potentially driving efficiency in operational decarbonization and green steel efforts." Macquarie has a "neutral" rating and A$15.00 target on Fortescue, which ended Thursday at A$15.89. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2234 GMT - After attending a Wesfarmers strategy briefing, Jefferies analysts Michael Simotas and Naveed Fazal Bawa aren't so sure about the Aussie conglomerate's recent M&A. They say recent additions, including setting up a lithium business and a health division, have been "suboptimal so far," and they point out the lithium business is now poised to book a greater than expected loss this fiscal year. The Jefferies analysts also say that M&A was downplayed at the briefing, with management appearing to prioritize organic capital allocation. Still, the Jefferies analysts, who have a "hold" rating on Wesfarmers, say that prospects for the company's main businesses, such as retailers Bunnings, Kmart, and Officeworks, remain strong. (mike.cherney@wsj.com)

1934 GMT - Live cattle futures on the CME jumped back over the $2.10 per pound threshold in today's session, with the most-active contract retracing its steps back toward its all-time high of roughly $2.17 a pound reached earlier this month. Expectations for tomorrow's Cattle on Feed report to show shrinking inventories, marketings, and new placements onto feedlots is supporting futures, StoneX says in a note. The report is scheduled to be published at 3 p.m. eastern time by the USDA. Lean hogs closed down 0.6% to $1.0195 a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1915 GMT - Oil futures grind lower with geopolitical news taking a back seat and the possibility of another large output increase by OPEC+ for July raising supply concerns. Citi analysts see "highly binary geopolitical risks" around U.S. negotiations on Iran's nuclear program and the Russia-Ukraine war. "Geopolitics remain pivotal for the next move in oil up to $70 Brent, or down to the $50s," they say. Citi expects OPEC+ to hold output at June levels through 2H25, "but another three-month slug could put pressure back on the market." Meanwhile, the bank keeps its 0-3 month Brent view at $60. WTI settles down 0.6% at $61.20 a barrel, and Brent falls 0.7% to $64.44. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1907 GMT [Dow Jones]--A lack of clarity on tariffs has pulled U.S. new-crop export sales down, CoBank said, adding the ongoing uncertainty is clouding the outlook for U.S. grain elevators and merchandisers. A research brief from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange said "the longer the uncertainty continues to drag on sales of new-crop exports, export-dependent grain elevators and merchandisers risk entering the 2025/26 marketing year with greater reliance on local demand, which may be scarce in some regions." As of May 1, U.S. new-crop export sales were below five-year historical averages, with soybeans falling 88.2% and corn dropping 26.9%. CoBank said. "Many foreign buyers have reduced forward coverage and are buying in the spot market, complicating the picture for elevators and merchandisers that are heavily reliant on export demand," CoBank said. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1903 GMT - Shifting U.S. trade policies, with tariffs on various partners still a threat, may slow the flow of export sales out of the U.S. this year, CoBank says in a note. Grain elevators, which buy grains from farmers to sell to domestic and foreign customers, are bracing for a smaller supply this year as farmers are unsure about more turmoil hitting crop prices. Farmers may opt to hold onto their harvested grains and not put them into the supply chain until that uncertainty eases. "For grain elevators and merchandisers that typically book grain sales out several months in advance, the headwinds of trade uncertainty remain firmly in place," the firm says. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Settle Lower

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Bond Market Shows Signs of Stabilizing
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

Germany GDP; France consumer confidence; U.K. retail sales; no major corporate trading updates expected

Opening Call:

European stock futures rose early Friday. Asian stock benchmarks advanced; the dollar weakened; Treasury yields were little changed; while oil futures edged lower and gold inched higher.

Equities:

European stock futures were higher as worries about a global bond selloff eased.

A selloff in global sovereign bonds this week took investors by surprise, although none of the issues they appeared to be taking issue with are recent developments.

While countries like the U.S. and Japan have carried heavy debt loads for decades, Moody's decision to strip the U.S. of its top-tier credit rating earlier this month appeared to set the selloff in motion.

With U.S. growth expectations souring and the Federal Reserve on the sidelines, it appears that the selling pressure on bonds could persist, said Jens Nordvig of Exante Data.

Forex:

Markets' reaction to rising fiscal uncertainty in the U.S. might be more harmful to the dollar than to Treasurys, Deutsche Bank's Tim Baker said. "Treasuries may get some eventual support as domestics rotate away from equities, but the retreat from foreigners would still play dollar-negative."

Bonds:

Treasury yields edged lower Thursday afternoon, offering reprieve from a selloff that first gained steam on Monday, after Moody's stripped the U.S. of its triple-A credit rating last week.

"Just like with the tariffs last month, we are about to find out what level of bond yields and the S&P 500 are necessary for Trump and Congress to course-correct on what is completely unsustainable fiscal policy," said Peter Berezin, chief global strategist at BCA Research.

Energy:

Oil fell early Friday, dragged lower by the possibility of another large output increase by OPEC+ for July.

Citi sees "highly binary geopolitical risks" around U.S. negotiations on Iran's nuclear program and the Russia-Ukraine war. "Geopolitics remain pivotal for the next move in oil up to $70 Brent, or down to the $50s."

Metals:

Gold edged higher in Asian trade, supported by lingering worries over ballooning U.S. deficits.

President Trump's "big, beautiful bill" is widely expected to add trillions of dollars to budget deficits in the coming years.

-

Inventories of key metals, such as copper, have been rising in recent weeks, suggesting that demand has waned, ANZ said. However, recent supply side issues could help limit the downside, it said.

-

Iron ore prices fell slightly, as consumption is declining in China. Weakening fundamentals are weighing on the ferrous metal's prices, Baocheng Futures said.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

U.K. Consumer Sentiment Edges Up From Low Mood as Tariff Turmoil Eases

Pessimism among U.K. consumers faded a little this month, likely a result of the rollback of some of U.S. trade tariffs and the Bank of England's most recent interest-rate cut, a survey published Friday said.

Consumer confidence improved three points to minus 20 in May, according to research group GfK's monthly index published with the Nuremberg Institute for Market Decisions.

Global Finance Officials Gloss Over Trade Tensions at G-7 Summit

BANFF, Alberta-Finance officials from some of the world's most powerful nations glossed over simmering tensions triggered by President Trump's far-reaching tariffs during an economic summit in this small ski town.

At the close of the three-day meeting, the Group of Seven finance and central bank chiefs issued a vague joint statement that offered few details about what was discussed at the meetings and the commitments countries were prepared to make on trade. The joint statement mentioned the word "trade" once and made no mention of tariffs, despite the topic being a focal point in private discussions at the conference.

The Deficit Is Unsettling Bond Traders. Here's How That Affects the Economy.

An economy facing possible indigestion from big increases in tariffs now is contending with a second headwind: potential fallout from a rise in government borrowing costs.

Congressional Republicans and the White House have plowed ahead with a package of tax cuts designed to shore up the economy and soothe jitters about the hit from tariffs. But the cost of the tax bill could be adding to investor concerns over perpetually high budget deficits.

Stellantis to Postpone Production of Electric Dodge Charger Model at Ontario Plant

Stellantis said it would delay production of its 2026 base-model electric Dodge Charger Daytona at its plant in Windsor, Ontario, while it assesses the effects of the Trump administration's tariff policies.

The automaker said other variants of the car remain on track at the plant, and the decision to postpone production of the Charger Daytona R/T will have no effect on jobs at the Windsor facility.

G-7 Makes Compromises on Trade to Reach United Front, EU Official Says

BANFF, Alberta-The Group of Seven finance and central bank chiefs have agreed on wording for a communiqué although there were compromises regarding global trade, says the European Union's top economic official.

"We managed to find a lot of common ground," Valdis Dombrovskis, the EU's commissioner for economy and productivity, told reporters on the sidelines of the G-7 meeting in the Rocky Mountain resort town of Banff. The communiqué is scheduled for release later Thursday.

Big Banks Explore Venturing Into Crypto World Together With Joint Stablecoin

The nation's biggest banks are exploring whether to team up to issue a joint stablecoin, a step intended to fend off escalating competition from the cryptocurrency industry.

The conversations have so far involved companies co-owned by JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo and other large commercial banks, according to people familiar with the matter. Those include Early Warning Services, the operator of the peer-to-peer payment system Zelle, and the Clearing House, the real-time payment network.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

06:00/SWE: Apr Labour Force Survey

06:00/SWE: 1Q Industrial inventories

06:00/GER: 1Q GDP - Detailed breakdown

06:00/UK: Apr UK monthly retail sales figures

06:30/HUN: Apr Employment & unemployment

06:45/FRA: May Consumer confidence survey

08:00/POL: Apr Agricultural prices

08:00/ICE: Apr PPI

10:00/FRA: 1Q OECD trade statistics release

12:00/POL: Apr Broad money M3

17:59/POR: Mar ICSG Copper Report

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Trump pushes EU to cut tariffs or face extra duties, FT reports
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's trade negotiators are pushing the EU to make unilateral tariff reductions on U.S. goods, saying without concessions the bloc will not progress in talks to avoid additional 20% "reciprocal" duties, the Financial Times reported on Friday.

Reuters was unable to immediately verify the report.

data.title: NetEase's Gaming Business Could Strengthen Growth Momentum — Market Talk
data.description: NetEase's gaming business could strengthen its growth momentum on a solid pipeline of new games and resilient legacy titles, Daiwa analysts say in a research note. At its annual game day, the Chinese videogame maker showcased upcoming titles such as "Marvel Mystic Mayhem" and "Destiny Rising," the analysts say. However, Daiwa expects NetEase to see margin volatility as cross-platform and international titles, which have lower margins, grow. However, overall profitability should remain stable through disciplined cost and resource management, they add. NetEase is well-positioned to benefit from industry trends toward blockbuster franchises and cross-platform games as it is prioritizing high-potential and long-lifecycle products, they add. While maintaining a buy call, Daiwa raises NetEase's H-share target price to HK$235.00 from HK$210.00. Shares are last at HK$190.10.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: India central bank reviewing shareholding norms for banks, says chief Malhotra
data.description:
India's central bank is examining foreign shareholding norms and licensing rules for banks as a part of a broader review, Governor Sanjay Malhotra said in an interview published by The Times of India on Friday.

"We are examining this in totality as part of our internal examination. We will review all aspects of the eligibility conditions," Malhotra told the newspaper when asked about increasing shareholding limits further, in particular for foreign banks wanting to come to India.

The Reserve Bank of India is also examining the need for scaling up banks to manage the increasing needs of the economy, the governor told the newspaper.

Malhotra refused to comment specifically on the troubles at private lender IndusInd Bank but said the system as a whole "is very robust and strong. There are no systemic risks or concerns."

data.title: Malaysia's Supply-Side Price Pressures May Remain Contained — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's supply-side price pressures are expected to stay contained through 1H, supported by weaker oil prices and a stronger ringgit, CIMB economists Azri Azhar and Michelle Chia say in a note. With CPI still above the PPI, businesses have little need to raise consumer prices, they say. However, they maintain their call for a 25bps cut by Malaysia's central bank in July, bringing the policy rate to 2.75%, citing fading growth momentum. Manufacturing PMI remains in contraction, reflecting persistent weakness in the manufacturing activity, they note. CIMB views the recent export strength as temporary due to frontloading activity amid the 90-day U.S. tariff pause. Although trade talks and a possible agreement with the U.S. are underway, the overall outlook remains highly uncertain, it adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Kossan Rubber Industries Target Price Raised to MYR1.62 from MYR1.54 by RHB IB >7153.KU
Kossan Rubber Industries Raised to Neutral From Sell by RHB IB >7153.KU
StanChart to Redeem EUR1 Billion Notes due 2030
data.description:
Standard Chartered said Thursday it will fully redeem 1 billion euros of 2.5% fixed-rate reset dated subordinated notes due 2030.

The notes will be redeemed on June 9 at the call option redemption amount, along with accrued and unpaid interest. The redeemed notes will be subsequently canceled and delisted from the London bourse's main market on June 10.

data.title: Bendigo Bank Showing Margin Discipline Amid Headwinds — Market Talk
data.description: Bendigo & Adelaide Bank's third-quarter update shows margin discipline in the face of lower interest rates and increased competition, Jarden analysts say. They think that the regional Australian lender did well to hold its net interest margin flat on the prior three months, but see compression resuming over coming periods against the backdrop of central-bank rate cuts. Bendigo's headline profit was about 2% stronger than the Jarden analysts had anticipated, which they tell clients in a note was due to benign credit quality. Jarden raises its target price 0.9% to A$11.60 and stays neutral on the stock, which is flat at A$11.77. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: India's Sun Pharma drops on US sales slowdown, weak FY26 outlook
data.description:
** Drugmaker Sun Pharma's shares decline 3.7% to 1,656 rupees

** Stock biggest drag on pharma index which is down 0.9%

** Co reports higher adjusted profit, but revenue below estimates as US sales drop 2.5%

** U.S. sales hurt by lower contribution from generic Revlimid due to weak pricing, says Nomura

** SUN forecasts "mid-to-high" single-digit percentage revenue growth for FY26 amid macro uncertainties

** JP Morgan analysts call SUN's outlook "cautious amidst global uncertainty"

** HSBC analysts expect SUN's margins to be pressured in FY26 on increasing costs related to launch of drugs Leqselvi and Unloxcyt

** Avg rating on SUN, Cipla at "buy", Dr Reddy's rated "hold" - data compiled by LSEG

data.title: Patel Engineering Secures Irrigation Project Worth INR9.58 Billion
data.description:
Patel Engineering , along with its joint venture partner, has received a letter of acceptance for an irrigation project worth 9.58 billion Indian rupees from Maharashtra Krishna Valley Development Corporation, according to a Friday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Patel Engineering's share in the contract is 1.92 billion rupees as it has a 20% stake in the joint venture.

The project is for the construction of a pipeline distribution network for Nira Deoghar Right Bank Main Canal and its distributaries in the Satara district of Maharashtra, India.

data.title: Cenergy's Steel Pipes Arm Books Order from Snam in Italy
data.description:
Cenergy said Thursday its Corinth Pipeworks business booked an order for 180 kilometers of steel pipes from Italian infrastructure company Snam (SRG.MI).

The order will be used for the Adriatica project, an infrastructure development to support Italy's natural gas network, the Belgian power transmission company noted.

data.title: Street View: Path improving for India's ITC after 'in-line' Q4
data.description:
** ITC reports higher Q4 profit on cigarettes business growth, rural market strength

** Stock up 1.2% to 431 rupees

** Of 37 analysts covering stock, at least seven cut PTs post results, three downgraded stock and five upgraded, per data compiled by LSEG

PRESSURES HAVE PEAKED, UPWARDS FROM HERE

** JP Morgan ("overweight"; PT 475 rupees): Q4 core profit in line with brokerage's estimate; better-than-expected cigarette sales volume growth

** Goldman Sachs ("buy"): Cigarette sales growth "steady"; margin pressure across cigarettes and FMCG "has peaked"

** BofA Securities ("buy", PT 470 rupees): Expects reversal of inflation trends in FMCG, cyclical downturn in paper segment over next 2-3 months to drive better earnings growth

** Investec ("buy", cuts PT to 493 rupees from 498 rupees): Expects ITC to get back to double digit earnings growth in FY26 with sequential improvement across segments

** Sees lower margins in FMCG, cigarettes, but risk reward is favorable

data.title: Brambles U.S. Margins Could Be Pressured by Lumber Inflation — Market Talk
data.description: Pallet giant Brambles's U.S. earnings margins could come under pressure from increased lumber prices, Jarden analysts say. They say in a note that lumber costs are reaccelerating at a time when pallet prices are stuck in reverse. Their analysis suggests a 10% on-year rise in lumber costs so far in Brambles' fiscal second half, compared with a 1.8% on-year decline in April for pallet prices. All told, they see broad near-term challenges for Brambles but maintain a neutral rating and its target price of A$19.80 on the stock. Shares are down 0.1% at A$22.13. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: NetEase H-Share Target Price Raised to HK$235.00 From HK$210.00 by Daiwa >NTES
Pierer Mobility's Majority Shareholder Enters Call Option for Future Change of Control
data.description:
Pierer Mobility (PKTM.VI, ) said its majority shareholder, Pierer Bajaj, entered into a call option that allows a change of control in the Austrian motorcycle and e-bikes manufacturer.

Pierer Industrie, which holds a 50.1% stake in Pierer Bajaj, concluded the call option with Bajaj Auto International Holdings, which holds the remaining 49.9% stake, according to a Thursday release.

The agreement allows Bajaj Auto International to gain indirect control over Pierer Mobility until the end of May 2026 by acquiring Pierer Industrie's shares in Pierer Bajaj.

The company noted that the move would require regulatory approvals.

data.title: Patel Engineering Says Co In JV Bags Irrigation Project
data.description:

data.title: Sunway Likely Has Clear Earnings Visibility With New Projects — Market Talk
data.description: Sunway Bhd. likely has clear earnings visibility due to its new development projects, RHB IB analyst Loong Kok Wen writes in a note. The conglomerate has also secured construction contracts, she says. Sunway's 1Q property sales of MYR554.7 million is on track to achieve its target of MYR3.6 billion by the year-end, she adds. New launches worth a total of MYR4.1 billion will be rolled out this year, she notes. RHB maintains the stock's buy rating and a target price of MYR5.79. Shares are 1.7% lower at MYR4.75.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: China 10-Year Yield Rises
data.description:
China’s 10-year government bond yield rose to around 1.70% on Friday, as investors shifted toward riskier assets following news that the US and China had agreed to sustain diplomatic dialogue amid persistent tensions across various fronts.

According to China’s Foreign Ministry, Vice Foreign Minister Ma Zhaoxu and US Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau held a phone call on Thursday to discuss key bilateral issues, a conversation later confirmed by the US State Department.

Investors viewed the renewed diplomatic engagement as a notable step toward managing the complex relationship between the two global powers.

Meanwhile, China’s central bank injected CNY 500 billion into the banking system via its one-year medium-term lending facility, aiming to maintain ample liquidity and support credit growth amid a fragile economic recovery.

The move follows the PBOC’s first key lending rate cut in seven months, lowering the one-year LPR to 3.0% and the five-year to 3.5%.

data.title: A Taiwanese producer offers Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in Bangladesh
data.description: A Polymerupdate source in Bangladesh stated that producer from Taiwan is heard to have offered its PVC Suspension grade at the USD 700/mt levels. This offer is for June 2025 shipment on CFR Chattogram port basis.
